finding: §4.1 states that in --merge and --resolve/--resolve-all modes 'any positional argument is rejected' and 'tp accepts exactly zero positionals in those modes'. But the project's own command reference (CLAUDE.md) documents --resolve/--resolve-all as taking positionals: `tp review --resolve findings.ndjson <idx> <disposition> "evidence"`. §4.3 then refers to 'the --resolve usage line' without ever giving it. The spec never defines how file/idx/disposition/evidence ARE passed under the new zero-positional rule (flags? which flags?), so two engineers cannot write the same positive (correct-invocation) test for --resolve — only the §19.3 rejection test is writable. The phrase 'the mode's expected argument shape' names a shape that is never specified.
suggestion: Add the canonical --resolve / --resolve-all usage line in §4 (e.g. flag forms like --file/--index/--disposition/--evidence, or a documented positional grammar that excludes the spec positional), and reconcile it with the 'zero positionals' statement so the positive invocation is pinned and testable.

finding: §5.1a says tp 'stages and commits the task file still in its pre-close (wip) state' to produce C1, but §5.1c calls C1 'the implementation commit whose tree holds the audited source files' and §11.1 derives suggested_files from 'paths touched by the commits recorded in commit_shas'. A commit that stages only the task file cannot also hold the audited implementation files; if C1 contained only the task file, §11.1's suggested_files would be empty (or just the task file) and §5.1c's rationale ('C1's tree holds the audited source files') would be false. The staging step omits the implementation files entirely, so an implementer reading §5.1a literally would produce a C1 that contradicts §5.1c and breaks §11.1.
suggestion: State in §5.1a that tp stages the implementation files (selected via --files or auto-detected, as today) together with the pre-close task file into C1 — e.g. 'tp stages the implementation files and the task file (still in its pre-close/wip state), commits them as the implementation commit (C1), then writes the closure record into the task file.'

finding: §9.1 states a skipped role's reason is "one of `no-checklist-items`, `no-spec-change`, or `domain-mismatch`" as an exhaustive enumeration, but §10.3 introduces a fourth value `no-baseline` for the round-1 regression role. An implementer building the §9.1 reason enum/validator against the three-value list would reject `no-baseline`, and the two sections disagree on the legal reason vocabulary.
suggestion: Make §9.1's list explicitly non-exhaustive ("includes, among others") or add `no-baseline` to it, so §9.1 and §10.3 agree on the full reason set.

finding: §10.1 reports `rounds_remaining` ('null when uncapped') and §10.2 reports `in_flight_round` for a started-but-unrecorded round, but the spec never defines how rounds_remaining is computed relative to an in-flight round. When max_rounds=3, two rounds recorded, and round 3 in-flight (snapshot written, no review-round file), is rounds_remaining 0 (in-flight consumes budget) or 1 (only recorded rounds consume)? Two engineers would write different assertions for the same fixture, and the §19.12 acceptance test touches both fields without pinning their interaction.
suggestion: Define rounds_remaining as a function of max_rounds, recorded rounds, and the in-flight round (e.g. 'max_rounds minus the count of recorded rounds; an in-flight round is not counted until recorded'), and state it in §10.1 or §10.2.

finding: Acceptance item 10 asserts 'merge and status output carry attribution_excludes (§9.1, §9.2)' unconditionally, but §9.2 makes attribution_excludes conditional: it is added 'only when that exclusion actually causes merged_count to exceed the overlap-report finding count' and 'when the counts already match the field is omitted (and it is always omitted under --compact)'. A test written from item 10 as-is would assert presence of attribution_excludes on every merge/status output and fail in the common case where the regression exclusion does not cause a count mismatch.

resolved.evidence: acceptance item 10 reworded at impl to match §9.2 condition

suggestion: Reword item 10 to match §9.2's condition — e.g. 'merge and status output carry attribution_excludes iff the regression exclusion causes merged_count to exceed the overlap-report finding count (§9.2)' — and pin the test fixture to that specific scenario.

finding: When no `-o` is given and stdout is piped, §3.1 streams findings to stdout AND emits the summary object on stdout, but never states the order or how a consumer separates summary lines from finding lines. The merge→record chain pipes this stdout into a file for `--record`; a summary object mixed into the findings becomes an "incomplete" line that §3.2 skips with a warning — the round still counts clean only by accident, and a consumer parsing stdout as a pure findings stream has no documented terminator.

resolved.evidence: stdout order findings-first then summary; impl pins

suggestion: Pin the order (findings first, summary object last) and/or require `-o` for the findings-when-piped case so stdout carries at most one of the two shapes.

finding: §3.2 defines a merge input line as "incomplete" if it is "valid JSON missing a required finding field", but never enumerates which fields are required for the completeness check. An implementer must guess the set (role? location? severity? finding? suggestion? all of them?) and two implementations could reject different line sets, changing `merged_count`.
suggestion: List the required finding fields explicitly in §3.2 (aligned with the finding output contract) so the incomplete-line check is deterministic.

finding: §3.2 defines an 'incomplete' line as 'valid JSON missing a required finding field' and says malformed/incomplete lines are skipped with a warning, but the spec never enumerates which fields are 'required' for a finding line. The finding contract (role/location/class/severity/finding/suggestion) is implied by the review prompt, not stated in the spec. A tester cannot construct a deterministic 'incomplete line' fixture without knowing the required set, and two engineers could disagree on whether, e.g., omitting `class` or `suggestion` triggers the skip+warning.
suggestion: List the required finding fields inline in §3.2 (e.g. 'a line is incomplete if it lacks any of role, location, severity, finding') so the skip predicate and the warning text are both testable.

finding: §5.1a says "tp stages and commits the task file still in its pre-close state" — naming only the task file as the staged content — while §5.1c calls C1 "the implementation commit whose tree holds the audited source files". Taken literally, §5.1a produces a C1 whose tree carries no implementation source, which then breaks §11.1's `suggested_files` (it reads C1's tree for paths to audit). The staging scope of C1 is stated two incompatible ways.
suggestion: State in §5.1a that C1 stages the implementation source files (per the existing `tp commit` staging rule) together with the pre-close task file, so §5.1a and §5.1c agree on what C1's tree contains.

finding: Guard (ii) requires the `git diff --name-only HEAD` set to be "tp-owned paths written during this close", but the spec gives no mechanism by which tp knows a dirty tp-owned path was written *by this command* rather than already dirty before it ran (e.g. a manually-edited `.tp/config.json`). Implementing the guard as "all dirty paths match tp-owned prefixes" would fold such a pre-existing tp-owned change into the amend, silently committing state tp did not write; the strict reading requires tp to snapshot the dirty set or track its own writes, which is undescribed.
suggestion: Pin the attribution: either tp records the paths it wrote during the command and checks the diff is a subset of those (strict), or state explicitly that any tp-owned dirty path is accepted (and acknowledge the fold-in consequence), so the guard's semantics are unambiguous.

finding: §5.2 defines bookkeeping as covering 'uncommitted files that are tp's own state (<base>.tasks.json, anything under .tp-review/, anything under .tp/)' but restricts each entry's `kind` to exactly `closure` or `round`. A dirty tp-owned file that is neither — e.g. `.tp/config.json`, `.tp/local.json`, or a future tp state file under `.tp/` — is in scope per the intro sentence but has no `kind` value assigned by the enum, so a tester cannot predict whether such a file appears in `bookkeeping` (and with what kind) or is silently dropped. This also blocks the §19.5 test if extended to non-closure tp state.
suggestion: Either add a `kind` value for generic tp-owned state (e.g. `config`) or explicitly state that only the task file and .tp-review/ round files are reported and other dirty tp-owned paths are ignored — then pin the behavior in acceptance.

finding: §5.2 scopes bookkeeping to 'uncommitted files that are tp's own state (<base>.tasks.json, anything under .tp-review/, anything under .tp/)' but defines the kind field as only 'closure' (the task file) or 'round' (a .tp-review/ file). A dirty file under .tp/ that is not a round file — e.g. .tp/config.json, .tp/.gitignore, a .tp/reviewers/*.json role file, or any future .tp/ state — is in bookkeeping scope but has no valid kind value. The spec does not say whether tp omits such a path (leaving it to surface as an unexplained-changes blocker, defeating §5.2's purpose) or emits it with a null/invalid kind (a schema violation).
suggestion: Either extend the kind enum with a third value (e.g. 'config' for non-round .tp/ state) with its ref semantics, or narrow bookkeeping's scope sentence to the two path classes the enum actually covers (task file + .tp-review/) and state explicitly that other .tp/ files are reported via the existing changes/unexplained-changes path.

finding: §6.1 says that in --bulk/--stdin 'validation runs after the whole batch is staged', and lists seven per-entry rejection rules all exiting 1, but never states the batch's failure semantics: if one entry in a 5-entry batch violates a rule, is the entire batch rejected (exit 1, zero tasks written) or are valid entries committed and only the bad one rejected? 'Staged then validated' could mean rollback-on-fail or skip-bad-keep-good. The §19.7 acceptance test only covers single-entry rejection, so a tester has no pinned behavior for the multi-entry partial-failure case.
suggestion: State explicitly whether --bulk/--stdin is atomic (whole batch fails on any bad entry) or partial (bad entries rejected, good ones written), and add a multi-entry acceptance case covering the chosen semantics.

finding: §8.4 claims "each new field states whether it survives" `--compact`, and enumerates the compact disposition for every new field introduced in §3–§16 — `bookkeeping`, `skipped_roles`, `attribution_excludes`, `overlap_report`, `max_rounds`/`rounds_remaining`/`in_flight_round`, and the report `note` — but omits `suggested_files` (§11.1). `suggested_files` is decision-critical (it is the file list the agent branches on to decide what to audit), so by §8.4's own rule it belongs in the "stays" column alongside `bookkeeping`. Leaving its compact behavior unspecified creates an AX hazard: tp's P3 principle pushes agents toward `--compact`, and if an implementation interprets the silence as "strip," the audit exit-4 payload loses the inline file list — the exact round-trip §11.1 was designed to eliminate — forcing the agent to either re-derive paths from git or make an extra `tp audit --affected-from-tasks` call.
suggestion: Add `suggested_files` to §8.4's "stays" list: '...decision-critical fields stay under `--compact` — `bookkeeping` (the agent must commit them), `suggested_files` (the agent branches on them to select audit targets), and `max_rounds`/`rounds_remaining`/`in_flight_round`...'. This closes the self-imposed completeness claim and guarantees §11.1's round-trip removal holds in the compact mode AX principles favor.

finding: §9.1 enumerates the three skip reasons but never defines the condition that maps to each — when a role is classified `no-checklist-items` vs `no-spec-change` vs `domain-mismatch` is left for the implementer to invent. `no-spec-change` in particular has no stated trigger (is it exclusive to `--diff-from`? does it compare the role's focus/domains against the changed sections?).
suggestion: Add a one-line trigger per reason (e.g. `no-checklist-items`: role focus/checklist empty; `no-spec-change`: `--diff-from` active and role focus outside changed sections; `domain-mismatch`: role domain ≠ file domain).

finding: §9.1 fixes skipped_roles.reason to a closed enumeration of three values: 'no-checklist-items', 'no-spec-change', or 'domain-mismatch'. §10.3 then adds a fourth value — reason 'no-baseline' — for the regression role on round 1, without updating §9.1's enumeration. The schema as specified in §9.1 would reject the value §10.3 emits (or vice versa, an implementer following §10.3 violates the §9.1 contract). The two sections disagree on the allowed reason set.
suggestion: Add 'no-baseline' to §9.1's enumeration (making it the canonical four-value list) and have §10.3 reference that list, so the reason enum is defined in exactly one place.

finding: §9.1 defines skipped_roles reason as a closed three-value set — "one of `no-checklist-items`, `no-spec-change`, or `domain-mismatch`". The round-2 revision added §10.3's round-1 regression handling (to resolve the snapshot-round-0.md gap), which emits a fourth value: the regression role at round 1 "is added to `skipped_roles` with reason `no-baseline`". The two sections now contradict each other on the allowed values of `reason`: a tester writing an enum-validity assertion from §9.1 would reject the very round-1 skip §10.3 mandates, while a tester reading §10.3 would accept it. This regression was introduced by the revision that resolved the prior round-1-baseline finding.

resolved.evidence: trigger=merged_count exceeds sum of overlap_report[].unique; impl pins

suggestion: Add `no-baseline` to §9.1's enumerated set so the defining section covers the round-1 case — e.g. "with `reason` one of `no-checklist-items`, `no-spec-change`, `domain-mismatch`, or `no-baseline` (the last only for the regression role at round 1, which has no `snapshot-round-0.md`)".

finding: §9.2 says attribution_excludes is added 'only when that exclusion actually causes merged_count to exceed the overlap-report finding count'. But the 'overlap-report finding count' is not a named, computable field: the overlap_report shape (§9.1/§9.3) is per-role {role, unique, shared, trim_candidate}, with no single documented total. A tester cannot deterministically compute the trigger condition (merged_count > <???>) to decide whether the field must be present or absent for a given fixture, so the §19.10 acceptance test is not writable as stated.
suggestion: Pin the comparison term — e.g. 'merged_count exceeds the sum of overlap_report[].unique' or name a concrete field like `overlap_report_total` — so the presence/absence of attribution_excludes is a deterministic, testable predicate.

finding: §5.1d defines the follow-up fallback for TWO distinct guard failures — (i) HEAD moved and (ii) a non-tp-owned path differing — but §19.4 only exercises the fallback 'when HEAD moved' (guard i). The guard-(ii) fallback (dirty non-tp path triggers chore(tp) follow-up that stages only tp-owned paths) has no acceptance case, so a tester following §19 would not cover half of the §5.1d contract.

resolved.evidence: guard-ii fallback acceptance added at impl; §5.1d defines it

suggestion: Add an acceptance item (or extend §19.4) asserting that a dirty non-tp-owned path at amend time also triggers the follow-up commit and leaves that non-tp path untouched (git status clean for tp-owned paths only).

finding: §5.1c states '§11.1's suggested_files reads C1's tree', but §11.1 defines suggested_files as 'the union of paths touched by the commits recorded in commit_shas' — i.e. the commit's diff (git diff-tree / show --name-only), not its full tree (git ls-tree). An implementer who takes 'reads C1's tree' literally would list every file in the repo at C1 rather than just the implementation files C1 touched, producing a wildly inflated suggested_files set. §11.1 is authoritative and correct; the §5.1c gloss is the misleading term.
suggestion: In §5.1c, replace 'reads C1's tree' with 'reads the paths C1 touched' (or 'derives from C1's diff') so it matches §11.1's 'paths touched by the commits' wording.

finding: §5.2 says any dirty `.tp-review/` file becomes a `bookkeeping` entry of `kind: round` with `ref` = the round number parsed from the filename, but §10.2 places both `snapshot-round-N.md` and `review-round-N.ndjson` under `.tp-review/<base>/`. The `kind` vocabulary (`closure`/`round`) cannot distinguish a dirty snapshot from a dirty round file, and it is unspecified whether a dirty snapshot produces a `round` entry or is ignored.
suggestion: State whether dirty snapshot files are bookkept (and if so, give them their own `kind`, e.g. `snapshot`) or excluded from the array.

finding: §9.1 enumerates skipped_roles reasons as three values (no-checklist-items, no-spec-change, domain-mismatch), while §10.3 introduces a fourth value (no-baseline) for the regression role at round 1. The full reason vocabulary is therefore split across two sections, so a tester reading §9.1 alone writes an enum-closedness assertion ('reason ∈ {3 values}') that fails on the round-1 regression case. The reason set is normative test data but is not consolidated.
suggestion: Collect the complete skipped_roles reason enum in one place (§9.1) and have §10.3 reference it, so the testable vocabulary is stated once and in full.
